cards.grammarNote:
Arabic has no copula (verb 'to be') in the present tense.
**الماء بارد** (al-ma' barid) literally means 'the-water cold' — no verb.
This nominal sentence structure is standard: subject + predicate adjective, no linking verb needed.

**خبز** (khubz) = bread — a collective/mass noun in Arabic, referring to bread as a substance.
From root **خبز** (kh-b-z), meaning to bake.
**يخبز** (yakhbuz) = he bakes, **خباز** (khabbaz) = baker — same root, different patterns.
**خبزة** (khubza) = a single loaf/piece of bread — add **ة** to get the unit noun from the collective.

**أرز** (aruzz) = rice — a mass/collective noun with no common plural in Arabic.
Note the double **زز** (zz) — this gemination (shadda) is shown as **أرُزّ** (aruzz) in fully vocalized text.
**مع الخضروات** (ma'a al-khadrawat) = with the vegetables — **خضروات** is the plural of **خضار** (vegetables).

**حليب** (halib) = milk — from root **حلب** (h-l-b), meaning to milk.
**حلب** is also the city Aleppo in Syria — the same root.
**حلّاب** (hallab) = milkman/dairy person. **محلب** (mahlab) = dairy.
In questions: **هل تريد** (hal turid) = do you want — **هل** is the yes/no question particle.

**بيضة** (bayda) = one egg — the **ة** (ta' marbuta) marks this as the unit noun.
**بيض** (bayd) = eggs (collective) — drop the **ة** to get the collective.
**بيضتان** (baydatan) = two eggs — dual form with **ـان** (-an) suffix.
**بيضتين** (baydatayn) = two eggs (oblique dual with **ـَين** suffix, used in phrases like **في الفطور**).
cards.englishPhrase: I eat two eggs for breakfast.

cards.grammarNote:
**جبن** (jubn) = cheese — a mass noun, no gender marking needed.
**جبن** also means cowardice in Arabic — same spelling, completely different root: **جَبُنَ** (jabuna, to be cowardly) vs **جُبْن** (jubn, cheese). Context resolves it.
**فيها جبن** (fiha jubn) = it has cheese in it — **في** (in) + **ها** (it, f.) is a useful construction.

**دجاج** (dajaj) = chicken (collective noun — as food or as the animal category).
**دجاجة** (dajaja) = one chicken — adding **ة** makes it a unit noun.
**الدجاج لذيذ** (ad-dajaj ladhidh) — note the sun letter assimilation: **ال + دجاج** → **الدجاج** — the **د** (d) is a sun letter, but it doesn't change the **ل** here because **د** is NOT one of the letters that assimilates.
Actually **د** is a sun letter — so it WOULD be pronounced 'ad-dajaj', assimilating the **ل** to **د** in pronunciation.

**لحم** (lahm) = meat — from root **لحم** (l-h-m), referring to flesh.
**لحم بقر** (lahm baqar) = beef (lit. 'cattle meat'), **لحم غنم** (lahm ghanam) = lamb.
**لحم** appears in the name **بيت لحم** (Bethlehem) — lit. 'house of meat/bread'.
**لا يأكل** (la ya'kul) = does not eat — **لا** negates a present-tense verb.

**سمك** (samak) = fish (collective noun — fish as a category or substance).
**سمكة** (samaka) = one fish — unit noun with **ة** suffix.
**سماك** (sammak) = fishmonger, **مصيدة** (masida) = fishing trap.
**أحب** (uhibb) = I love/like — from root **حب** (h-b-b). The **ال** (al-) on **السمك** makes it 'the fish' as a category: 'I like fish (in general)'.

**حمص** (hummus) = chickpeas / the dip made from them — the word literally means chickpeas.
The dish name is short for **حمص بالطحينة** (hummus bi-t-tahina) = chickpeas with tahini.
**أكل الحمص** (akl al-hummus) = eating hummus — verbal noun **أكل** (akl) from root **أكل** ('-k-l).
**مع الخبز** (ma'a al-khubz) = with bread — the preposition **مع** for accompaniment.
cards.englishPhrase: I love eating hummus with bread.

cards.grammarNote:
**فلافل** (falafel) = falafel — the word is already a broken plural form of **فلفل** (filfil, pepper/spice), though the connection to the dish is debated.
**طعام شعبي** (ta'am sha'bi) = popular/street food — **شعبي** means 'of the people'.
**الفلافل** + adjective: the **ال** makes it definite — 'falafel (as a food type) is popular street food'.
cards.englishPhrase: Falafel is a popular street food.

cards.grammarNote:
**خبز البيتا** (khubz al-bita) = pita bread — an إضافة (idafa) construction: 'bread of the pita'.
In إضافة, the first noun loses any definite article while the second takes **ال**.
**مع كل وجبة** (ma'a kull wajba) = with every meal — **كل** (kull) = every/all, followed by an indefinite singular noun.
cards.englishPhrase: We eat pita bread with every meal.
cards.phrase: نأكل خبز البيتا مع كل وجبة.
cards.phraseRomanized: naʾkul khubz al-bītā maʿa kull wajba.

cards.grammarNote:
**لحم خنزير** (lahm khinzir) is **حرام** (haram) — forbidden in Islam — and generally unavailable in most Arab countries.
Useful phrase: **لا آكل لحم الخنزير** (la akul lahm al-khinzir) = I don't eat pork.

**فاكهة** (fakiha) = fruit (collective/general term).
**الفاكهة** (al-fakiha) = fruit in general — with the definite article for a generic category.
**فواكه** (fawakiha) = fruits (broken plural) — used when listing or specifying types.
**كل يوم** (kull yawm) = every day — **كل** + indefinite singular for 'every'.

**تفاحة** (tuffaha) = one apple — unit noun with **ة** (ta' marbuta).
**تفاح** (tuffah) = apples / apple (collective) — drop the **ة** for the collective.
**في اليوم** (fi al-yawm) = a day / per day — lit. 'in the day'.
**مفيدة للصحة** (mufida lis-sihha) = good for health — **لـ** (for) + **الصحة** (health).
cards.englishPhrase: An apple a day is healthy.

cards.grammarNote:
**موز** (mawz) = bananas (collective) — **موزة** (mawza) = one banana.
The word is borrowed into Arabic, but follows standard Arabic collective/unit patterns.
**ناضج** (nadij) = ripe — adjective from root **نضج** (n-d-j), meaning to ripen/cook through.
**الموز ناضج** — equational sentence: subject (**الموز**) + predicate (**ناضج**), no verb.

**برتقال** (burtuqal) = oranges (collective) — from 'Portugal', where the fruit was historically associated.
**برتقالة** (burtuqala) = one orange — unit noun.
**عصير برتقال** (asir burtuqal) = orange juice — an إضافة: 'juice of orange'.
Note: no **ال** on **برتقال** here — the second noun in إضافة can be indefinite: 'juice of (some) orange'.

**خضروات** (khadrawat) = vegetables — a sound plural with the **ـات** (-at) feminine plural suffix.
**خضار** (khudar) = greens/produce — a collective synonym.
Root **خضر** (kh-d-r) = green — **أخضر** (akhdar) = green (color), **خضراء** (khadra') = green (f.).
**كُل خضرواتك** (kul khadrawatak) = eat your vegetables — **كُل** is the imperative of **أكل**, and **ك** suffix = your.

**بطاطس** (batatais) = potatoes — a borrowed word (from Spanish/Portuguese batata) that has been pluralized in Arabic.
**بطاطا** (batata) = another common form, especially in Levantine dialects.
**مهروسة** (mahrusa) = mashed — from root **هرس** (h-r-s), meaning to mash/crush.
**بطاطس مهروسة** = mashed potatoes — adjective follows noun and agrees in gender.

**طماطم** (tamatim) = tomatoes — a borrowed word (from Nahuatl tomatl via European languages).
**طماطة** (tamata) = one tomato in some dialects — though the plural form is more commonly cited.
**السلطة فيها طماطم** — **فيها** (fiha) = in it (f.) — the subject **السلطة** (salad) is feminine, so 'in it' uses the feminine suffix **ها**.

**بصل** (basal) = onions (collective) — **بصلة** (basala) = one onion.
**لا أحب البصل** = I don't like onions — **لا** negates the verb **أحب**.
The definite article **البصل** is used here for 'onions in general' — definite article for generic reference is common in Arabic.

**سلطة** (salata) = salad — borrowed from Italian/French 'salata/salade', itself from Latin 'salata' (salted things).
The word ends in **ة** (ta' marbuta) making it grammatically feminine.
**سآخذ** (sa-akhudh) = I will take — the prefix **سـ** (sa-) on a present-tense verb signals simple future.

**حساء** (hisa') = soup — from root **حسا** (h-s-w/y), meaning to sip/slurp.
**يحسو** (yahsu) = to sip. The root elegantly captures how one eats soup.
**ساخن** (sakhin) = hot — from root **سخن** (s-kh-n): **سخونة** (sukhuna) = heat, **مسخّن** (musakhkhan) = heated.
**الحساء ساخن** — another equational sentence, no verb needed.

**شطيرة** (shatira) = sandwich — from root **شطر** (sh-t-r), meaning to halve/split.
A sandwich is literally 'the split thing' — bread split open to fill.
**صنعت** (sana'tu) = I made — past tense, first person. Root **صنع** (s-n-') = to make/manufacture: **صناعة** (sina'a) = industry/manufacture.
**للغداء** (lil-ghada') = for lunch — **لـ** + **الغداء**.
cards.englishPhrase: I made a sandwich for lunch.

cards.grammarNote:
**كعكة** (ka'ka) = cake — a borrowed/ancient term for a baked sweet.
**كعك** (ka'k) = cookies/biscuits (collective) in some dialects.
The **ة** (ta' marbuta) marks it as feminine, which affects agreement: **كعكة كبيرة** (a big cake, f.).
**هل تريد** (hal turid) = do you want — **هل** is the yes/no question marker.

**بسكويت** (biskwit) = cookies/biscuits — borrowed from French 'biscuit' (itself from Latin bis coctus, twice baked).
In Arabic, borrowed words are treated as masculine by default unless the form suggests otherwise.
**لذيذ** (ladhidh) = delicious (m.) — the masculine form because **البسكويت** is masculine.

**شوكولاتة** (shukulata) = chocolate — borrowed from Spanish/French, ultimately from Nahuatl xocolātl.
The **ة** (ta' marbuta) makes it grammatically feminine.
**أحب الشوكولاتة** — **الشوكولاتة** is definite with **ال**, meaning 'chocolate in general' — using the definite article for generic reference.

**سكر** (sukkar) = sugar — the English word 'sugar' traces back directly to this Arabic word, via Old French sucre and Medieval Latin succarum.
The Arabic **سكر** came from Sanskrit शर्करा (sharkara).
**بدون سكر** (bidun sukkar) = without sugar — one of the most useful phrases at any Arab café.
**سكر** also means 'to become drunk' from a different root — same spelling, different meaning.

**ملح** (milh) = salt — from root **ملح** (m-l-h), also meaning salty/beautiful in some poetic uses.
**مالح** (malih) = salty — the adjective form.
**هل يمكنك تناولي الملح؟** = can you pass me the salt? — **تناولي** (tanawuli) here means 'to pass/hand to me', from **تناول** (to take/receive).

**فلفل** (filfil) = pepper — the word has a reduplicative structure (f-l-f-l), which is a pattern found in several Arabic words and suggests an early loanword.
**فلفل أسود** (filfil aswad) = black pepper, **فلفل أحمر** (filfil ahmar) = red pepper/paprika.
Note: **فلافل** (falafel) may be etymologically related — both involve round, peppery associations.

**زبدة** (zubda) = butter — from root **زبد** (z-b-d), meaning foam/cream/butter.
**زبد** (zabad) = foam/froth — what rises to the top, hence butter is 'the cream of the milk'.
**أضع** (ada') = I put — from root **وضع** (w-d-') meaning to place.
**على الخبز** ('ala al-khubz) = on the bread — **على** ('ala) = on/upon.
cards.englishPhrase: I put butter on my bread.

cards.grammarNote:
**زيت** (zayt) = oil — from root **زيت** (z-y-t), closely connected to **زيتون** (zaytun, olive).
**زيت الزيتون** (zayt az-zaytun) = olive oil — an إضافة (idafa): 'oil of the olive'.
**الزيتون** — note the sun letter: **ز** (z) is actually a sun letter, so **ال + زيتون** → **الزيتون** (az-zaytun), the **ل** assimilates to **ز**.

**قهوة** (qahwa) = coffee — the English word 'coffee' came from Arabic **قهوة** via Turkish kahve.
Originally **قهوة** referred to wine (something that dulled appetite), then transferred to coffee when it spread from Ethiopia/Yemen.
**في الصباح** (fi as-sabah) = in the morning — **ص** is a sun letter, so **الصباح** is pronounced *as-sabah*.
cards.englishPhrase: I like coffee in the morning.

cards.grammarNote:
**شاي** (shay) = tea — from Chinese chá, entering Arabic via Persian.
The English word 'tea' came from the Hokkien Chinese pronunciation (tê), while 'chai' came via the Mandarin/Arabic/Persian route.
**شاياً** (shayan) — the indefinite accusative with tanwin used in a formal sentence object position.
**هل تريد شاياً؟** — **هل** opens a yes/no question.

**نبيذ** (nabidh) = wine — from root **نبذ** (n-b-dh), meaning to cast/throw aside or to ferment (something set aside to ferment).
Classically **نبيذ** referred to date wine or any fermented drink; grape wine was **خمر** (khamr).
**خمر** (khamr) is the Quranic term for intoxicating drink, declared **حرام** (haram) in Islamic law.
**كوب نبيذ أحمر** = a glass of red wine — **أحمر** (ahmar, red) is a color adjective.
cards.englishPhrase: A glass of red wine, please.

cards.grammarNote:
**بيرة** (bira) = beer — a loanword from European languages (bière/beer).
The **ة** (ta' marbuta) makes it grammatically feminine.
**بيرتان** (birratan) = two beers — the dual form. For exactly two, Arabic uses the **ـان** (-an) dual suffix: **بيرة** → **بيرتان** (nominative dual). Note: in the sample phrase **بيرتان** is nominative, which is used when the noun is the subject.

**عصير** (asir) = juice — from root **عصر** ('-s-r), meaning to squeeze/press.
**عصر** (asr) = to squeeze, also 'age/era' — both the pressing of time and the pressing of fruit.
**العصر** (al-'asr) = the afternoon prayer — one of the five daily prayers.
**عصير برتقال** (asir burtuqal) = orange juice — إضافة: juice of orange.

**فطور** (futur) = breakfast — from root **فطر** (f-t-r), meaning to break fast or to split open.
**الفطر** (al-fitr) = breaking the fast — as in **عيد الفطر** (Eid al-Fitr), the celebration at the end of Ramadan when the fast is broken.
**في السابعة** (fi as-sabi'a) = at seven — **السابعة** is the feminine ordinal 'the seventh', used for telling time.

**غداء** (ghada') = lunch — from root **غدو** (gh-d-w), referring to the early part of the day or 'morrow'.
**نتناول** (natanawal) = we eat/have — from root **تناول** (t-n-w-l), meaning to take/receive/consume.
**تناول** is the formal word for eating a meal: **تناول الطعام** (to have food/a meal).
**في الثانية** (fi ath-thaniya) = at two (o'clock) — **الثانية** = the second (ordinal).

**عشاء** (asha') = dinner — from root **عشو** ('-sh-w), relating to evening/darkness.
**العشاء** is also the name of the night prayer (one of the five daily prayers in Islam).
**جاهز** (jahiz) = ready — a common adjective: **الطعام جاهز** (the food is ready). Feminine: **جاهزة** (jahiza).
Equational sentence: **العشاء جاهز** — no verb needed.

**وجبة خفيفة** (wajba khafifa) = snack — lit. 'a light meal'.
**وجبة** (wajba) = meal — from root **وجب** (w-j-b), meaning obligatory/necessary — a meal is what is necessary.
**خفيفة** (khafifa) = light (f.) — feminine because **وجبة** is feminine.
Opposite: **وجبة دسمة** (wajba dasima) = a heavy/filling meal.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a snack in the afternoon.
cards.phrase: آكل وجبة خفيفة في بعد الظهر.
cards.phraseRomanized: akul wajba khafifa fi ba'd adh-dhuhr.

cards.grammarNote:
**وجبة** (wajba) = meal — feminine noun (note the **ة** ending).
**وجبات** (wajbat) = meals — the sound feminine plural, formed by adding **ـات** (-at).
**هذه وجبة لذيذة** — **هذه** (hadhihi, this, f.) agrees with the feminine noun **وجبة**. **لذيذة** (ladhidha, delicious, f.) also agrees.

**وصفة** (wasfa) = recipe — from root **وصف** (w-s-f), meaning to describe/prescribe.
**وصف** (wasf) = description, **طبيب وصف** = a doctor prescribes — same root.
The word for a medical prescription is also **وصفة طبية** (wasfa tibbiyya) = medical prescription.
**هل عندك** (hal 'indak) = do you have — using **عند** for possession.
cards.englishPhrase: Do you have a good recipe?

cards.grammarNote:
**نكهة** (nakha) = flavor/taste — from root **نكه** (n-k-h), meaning aroma and flavor.
**أي** (ayy) = which/any — used to ask for a selection: **أي نكهة** (which flavor?).
**أي** is followed by an indefinite noun without **ال**: **أي كتاب** (which book?), **أي مكان** (which place?).

**مكوّن** (mukawwin) = ingredient/component — from root **كون** (k-w-n), meaning to be/exist/form.
**مكوّنات** (mukawwinat) = ingredients — sound feminine plural.
**التي تحتاجها** (allati tahtajuha) = that you need (f.) — **التي** is the feminine relative pronoun 'which/that', and **ها** at the end refers back to the feminine **المكونات**.

**قائمة الطعام** (qa'imat at-ta'am) = menu — lit. 'list of food'.
An إضافة construction: **قائمة** (list) + **الطعام** (the food).
**قائمة** comes from root **قوم** (q-w-m), meaning to stand/list — a list is what 'stands up' as an account.
**هل يمكنني** (hal yumkinuni) = can I / is it possible for me — impersonal **يمكن** + pronoun suffix **ني** (me).

**مطعم** (mat'am) = restaurant — from root **طعم** (t-'-m), meaning food/taste.
The **مَفْعَل** pattern (**مَطْعَم**) creates place nouns: a restaurant is 'a place of eating'.
Same pattern: **مطبخ** (matbakh, kitchen = place of cooking), **مكتب** (maktab, office = place of writing).
**هذا المطعم جيد** — **هذا** (this, m.) agrees with the masculine noun **مطعم**.

**مطبخ** (matbakh) = kitchen — from root **طبخ** (t-b-kh), meaning to cook.
**يطبخ** (yatbukh) = he cooks, **طبّاخ** (tabbakh) = chef/cook, **مطبوخ** (matbukh) = cooked — all from the same root.
**المطبخ نظيف** — equational sentence: 'the kitchen (is) clean'. **نظيف** (nadhif) = clean, from root **نظف** (n-z-f).

**فرن** (furn) = oven — also means bakery in many dialects.
**الفرن** is masculine — no **ة** ending, so adjectives agreeing with it are masculine: **ساخن** (sakhin, hot, m.).
Root **فرن** (f-r-n) relates to the heat of baking; the same word for oven is used for communal clay ovens in traditional Arab villages.

**موقد** (mawqid) = stove — from root **وقد** (w-q-d), meaning to kindle/ignite.
**وَقُود** (waqud) = fuel, **إيقاد** (iqad) = kindling/igniting — same root.
Another **مَفْعِل** pattern noun: **موقد** = the place/instrument of ignition.

**سكين** (sikkin) = knife — from root **سكن** (s-k-n), meaning to calm/settle/reside.
A knife 'settles' or 'silences' — an old semantic connection to cutting/stilling.
**سكين حاداً** (sikkinen haddan) — tanwin (double ending) shows indefinite accusative case in formal Arabic.
**حاد** (hadd) = sharp — from root **حدد** (h-d-d), meaning to sharpen/define.

**شوكة** (shawka) = fork — from root **شوك** (sh-w-k), meaning thorn/spike.
A fork is literally 'a thorn' or 'the thorned thing' — a vivid visual metaphor.
**شوك** (shawk) = thorns (collective), **شجرة شائكة** (shajara sha'ika) = a thorny tree.
**هل يمكنني** (hal yumkinuni) = may I / can I — polite impersonal construction.

**ملعقة** (mil'aqa) = spoon — from root **لعق** (l-'-q), meaning to lick.
A spoon is 'the licking instrument' — a very literal and memorable origin.
**يلعق** (yal'aq) = to lick. The **مِفْعَلَة** pattern creates instrument nouns: **ملعقة** = licking tool = spoon.

**طبق** (tabaq) = plate/dish — from root **طبق** (t-b-q), meaning to match/correspond/layer.
**مطابق** (mutabiq) = matching/corresponding, **طبقة** (tabaqa) = layer/stratum — all from the same root.
Plural: **أطباق** (atbaq) — a broken plural pattern **أَفْعَال**.
**الطبق متسخ** (at-tabaq mutassikh) = the plate is dirty — equational sentence.

**وعاء** (wi'a') = bowl/container — from root **وعى** (w-'-y), meaning to contain/hold/understand.
**وعي** (wa'y) = awareness/consciousness — the mind as a 'container' of knowledge.
**امل الوعاء** (amla' al-wi'a') = fill the bowl — **أملأ** (amla') = to fill, imperative here.

**كوب** (kub) = cup/glass — a common loanword found across many languages.
Dual: **كوبان** (kubani) = two cups (nominative) / **كوبَين** (kubayn) = two cups (genitive/accusative).
**كوب ماء** (kub ma') = a glass of water — إضافة: 'glass of water', no preposition needed.

**زجاجة** (zujaja) = bottle — from root **زجج** (z-j-j), relating to glass material.
**زجاج** (zujaj) = glass (material) — **زجاجة** is literally 'a glass thing' → bottle.
**زجاجة نبيذ** (zujajat nabidh) = a bottle of wine — إضافة: 'bottle of wine'. Note: in إضافة the first noun loses its tanwin.

**طاولة** (tawila) = table — a loanword adapted into Arabic with the feminine **ة** ending.
**طاولتنا** (tawilantuna) = our table — **طاولة** + **نا** (-na, our) possessive suffix.
When a possessive suffix is added to a word ending in **ة** (ta' marbuta), the **ة** is written as **ت**: **طاولة** → **طاولتنا**.

**نادل** (nadil) = waiter — from root **ندل** (n-d-l), meaning to serve/hand over.
**يا نادل** (ya nadil) = O waiter — **يا** (ya) is the vocative particle used to address someone directly.
**الفاتورة** (al-fatura) = the bill/invoice — from root **فتر** or borrowed, widely used in Arab restaurants.
**يا** is one of the most essential Arabic particles — used before any name or title when addressing someone.

Arabic expresses hunger and thirst with adjectives directly — no verb needed:
**أنا جائع** (ana ja'i') = I am hungry. **أنا عطشان** (ana atshan) = I am thirsty.
Feminine forms: **جائعة** (ja'i'a), **عطشانة** (atshanah).

**عطشان** (atshan) = thirsty — a colloquial adjective form widely used across dialects.
Formal MSA: **عطش** ('atish, m.) / **عطشى** ('atsha, f.).
The **ـان** (-an) ending is typical of many colloquial emotional-state adjectives: **جوعان** (hungry), **تعبان** (tired), **زعلان** (angry/upset), **فرحان** (happy).

**يأكل** (ya'kul) = he eats — root **أكل** ('-k-l).
**لنأكل** (lna'kul) = let's eat — **لـ** (li-) + **نأكل** (we eat) = 'let us eat'. This construction with **لـ** creates a hortative ('let's...').
**آكل** (akul) = I eat — first person. **نأكل** (na'kul) = we eat. **يأكل** (ya'kul) = he eats.

**يشرب** (yashrab) = he drinks — from root **شرب** (sh-r-b), meaning to drink.
**شراب** (sharab) = drink/beverage, **شارب** (sharib) = drinker/one who drinks, **مشروب** (mashrub) = a drink (lit. 'the drunk thing') — all from the same root.
**شيئاً للشرب** (shay'an lish-shurb) = something to drink — **للشرب** = for drinking, a verbal noun.
cards.englishPhrase: Would you like something to drink?

cards.grammarNote:
**يطبخ** (yatbukh) = he/she cooks — from root **طبخ** (t-b-kh).
**هي تطبخ** (hiya tatbukh) = she cooks — note the feminine verb prefix **تـ** (t-).
**جيداً جداً** (jayyidan jiddan) = very well — the adverb **جيداً** (well) comes before the intensifier **جداً** (very).
Double adverbs in Arabic: the manner adverb comes first, then the degree.

**يخبز** (yakhbuz) = he bakes — from root **خبز** (kh-b-z), meaning to bake bread.
**خباز** (khabbaz) = baker — the intensive agent pattern **فَعَّال**.
**يوم الأحد** (yawm al-ahad) = Sunday — lit. 'day of the first'. **الأحد** = the first/one, which is Sunday in the Arabic calendar.

**يقلي** (yaqli) = he fries — from root **قلى** (q-l-y), meaning to fry/roast.
**مقلي** (maqli) = fried — the passive participle: **بطاطس مقلية** (fried potatoes).
**اقلِ** (iqli) = fry! (imperative) — note the imperative of root-final weak verbs often ends in a kasra.
**في الزيت** (fi az-zayt) = in the oil — **الزيت** with sun-letter assimilation: **ز** is a sun letter, so **الزيت** → *az-zayt*.

**يشوي** (yashwi) = he roasts/grills — from root **شوى** (sh-w-y), meaning to grill/roast.
**مشوي** (mashwi) = grilled/roasted — the passive participle used as an adjective: **دجاج مشوي** (grilled chicken).
**لمدة ساعة** (li-mudat sa'a) = for the duration of an hour — **مدة** (mudda) = duration, **ساعة** (sa'a) = hour.
cards.englishPhrase: Roast the chicken for one hour.

cards.grammarNote:
**يقطع** (yaqta') = he chops/cuts — from root **قطع** (q-t-'), meaning to cut/sever.
**قطعة** (qit'a) = a piece/cut — the noun: **قطعة لحم** (a piece of meat).
**مقطّع** (muqatta') = chopped/cut up — intensive form showing repeated or thorough cutting.
Imperative: **اقطع** (iqta') = cut!/chop! (masculine singular).

**يشرّح** (yusharrah) = he slices — from root **شرح** (sh-r-h), meaning to explain/dissect/slice.
**شرح** (sharh) = explanation/commentary — the same root for explaining and slicing suggests the idea of opening something up to reveal its contents.
**تشريح** (tashrih) = dissection/anatomy — also from this root.

**يمزج** (yamzuj) = he mixes — from root **مزج** (m-z-j), meaning to mix/blend.
**مزيج** (mazij) = mixture — the noun form.
**جميع** (jami') = all/entire — from root **جمع** (j-m-'), meaning to gather/collect.
**جميع المكونات** = all the ingredients — **جميع** followed by a definite noun.

**يحرّك** (yuharrik) = he stirs/moves — from root **حرك** (h-r-k), meaning to move.
**حركة** (haraka) = movement/motion — the noun: **حركة المرور** (traffic).
**محرّك** (muharrik) = engine/motor (lit. 'the mover') — same root.
The doubled consonant in **يحرّك** signals Form II — the intensified/causative verbal pattern.

**يخفق** (yakhfuq) = he whisks/beats — from root **خفق** (kh-f-q), meaning to beat/flutter/throb.
**خفقان القلب** (khafaqan al-qalb) = heart palpitation — the same root, of a heart beating fast.
**البيض** (al-bayd) = the eggs — **بيض** is a broken plural (collective) of **بيضة**.

**يسكب** (yaskub) = he pours — from root **سكب** (s-k-b), meaning to pour/spill.
**انسكب** (insikab) = to be poured/spilled — the reflexive/passive Form VII.
**اسكب الماء في الكوب** — a typical imperative instruction. The preposition **في** (in) specifies the destination.
cards.englishPhrase: Pour the water into the glass.

cards.grammarNote:
**يقدّم** (yuqaddim) = he serves/presents — from root **قدم** (q-d-m), meaning to present/offer.
**قدّم** (qaddama) = he presented (past tense). **تقديم** (taqdim) = presentation/serving.
Root **قدم** also means 'foot' and 'to advance' — the semantic range covers moving forward and presenting.
**النادل قدّم الطعام** — past-tense equational-word-order: verb + subject + object.

**يطعم** (yut'im) = he feeds — from root **طعم** (t-'-m), meaning food/taste.
**يطعم** (Form IV) = to cause someone to eat/taste = to feed — the causative pattern.
**طعام** (ta'am) = food, **مطعم** (mat'am) = restaurant — all from the same root.

**يتناول** (yatanawal) = to have/consume — from root **نول** (n-w-l), meaning to reach/hand.
Form VI **تناول** = to partake of, to receive — a more formal/polished word for eating.
**تناولنا العشاء** (tanawalna al-asha') = we had dinner — past tense with **نا** suffix (we).
**في مطعم جيد** = at a good restaurant — **في** for location.
cards.englishPhrase: We dined at a nice restaurant.

cards.grammarNote:
**يمضغ** (yamdag) = he chews — from root **مضغ** (m-d-gh), meaning to chew.
**مضغة** (mudgha) = a chewed piece/morsel — the same root used in the Quran to describe a stage of embryonic development.
**ببطء** (bi-but') = slowly — **بـ** (bi-, with/in a manner of) + **بطء** (but', slowness).

**يبتلع** (yabtali') = he swallows — from root **بلع** (b-l-') meaning to swallow.
**ابتلع** (ibtalaa') = to swallow (Form VIII — the reflexive/intensive derived form).
**الحبة بالماء** (al-habba bil-ma') = the pill with water — **حبة** (habba) = pill/grain, **الماء** = the water.

**يتبّل** (yutabbil) = he seasons — from root **تبل** (t-b-l), meaning spice.
**تابل** (tabil) = spice, **توابل** (tawabil) = spices (broken plural).
**بالملح والفلفل** (bil-milh wal-filfil) = with salt and pepper — **بـ** (with) + **ال** (the) + **و** (and).

**طازج** (tazij) = fresh — from root **طزج** (t-z-j), meaning freshness.
**الخبز طازج** — equational sentence, no verb. Adjective after noun agrees in gender: **خبز** (m.) → **طازج** (m.).
Feminine: **طازجة** (tazija) — **اللحمة طازجة** (the meat is fresh).

**ناضج** (nadij) = ripe — from root **نضج** (n-d-j), meaning to ripen/mature/cook through.
**نضج** also means to become mature (of a person) — **إنسان ناضج** (a mature person).
Feminine: **ناضجة** (nadija) — **الطماطم ناضجة** (the tomatoes are ripe, f. pl.).

**مطبوخ** (matbukh) = cooked — the passive participle from root **طبخ** (t-b-kh).
The **مَفْعُول** pattern makes passive participles: **مطبوخ** = that which has been cooked.
Same pattern: **مكتوب** (written), **مشروب** (drunk/beverage), **مأكول** (eaten).
Feminine: **مطبوخة** (matbukha) — **الدجاجة مطبوخة** (the chicken is cooked, f.).

**ساخن** (sakhin) = hot (of a thing/temperature) — from root **سخن** (s-kh-n).
**سخونة** (sukhuna) = heat, **مسخّن** (musakhkhan) = heated/warmed up.
Don't confuse with **حارّ** (harr) = spicy/peppery hot — **ساخن** is temperature heat.
**انتبه** (intabih) = be careful/watch out — a useful warning word, from root **نبه** (n-b-h, to alert).
cards.englishPhrase: Be careful, the soup is hot.

cards.grammarNote:
**بارد** (barid) = cold — from root **برد** (b-r-d), meaning cold/to cool.
**برد** (bard) = cold (noun), **مبرّد** (mubarrad) = refrigerator/cooler.
Feminine: **باردة** (barida) — **قهوتي باردة** (qahwati barida) = my coffee is cold.
**قهوتي** (qahwati) = my coffee — **قهوة** + possessive suffix **ي** (-i, my), with **ة** → **ت** before the suffix.

**حلو** (hulw) = sweet — from root **حلو** (h-l-w), meaning sweetness.
**حلوى** (halwa) = sweets/confectionery — same root: **محل حلويات** (sweet shop).
Feminine: **حلوة** (hulwa) — the **ة** makes it agree with feminine nouns: **الكعكة حلوة** (the cake is sweet).
**هذه الكعكة** (hadhihi al-ka'ka) — **هذه** (this, f.) agrees with feminine **كعكة**.

**حامض** (hamid) = sour/acidic — from root **حمض** (h-m-d), meaning acid/sourness.
**حمض** (hamd) = acid — the same root used in chemistry: **حمض أميني** (amino acid).
**ليمون** (laymun) = lemon — borrowed word. **الليمون حامض** — equational sentence, no verb.

**مرّ** (murr) = bitter — from root **مرر** (m-r-r), meaning bitterness/to pass.
**مرارة** (marara) = bitterness — the abstract noun.
Feminine: **مرّة** (murra) — **هذه القهوة مرّة** (this coffee is bitter, f.).
Interestingly, **مرّ** also means 'he passed by' (a different voweling of the same root) — bitterness and passing time share a root.

**حارّ** (harr) = spicy/peppery hot — from root **حرر** (h-r-r), meaning heat/burning.
**حرارة** (harara) = heat/temperature, **محرار** (mihrar) = thermometer — same root.
Don't confuse with **ساخن** (sakhin) = hot in temperature — **حارّ** specifically means spicy/pungent.
Feminine: **حارّة** (harra) — **الصلصة حارّة** (the sauce is spicy).

**طريّ** (tariyy) = tender/soft/fresh — from root **طرو** (t-r-w/y), meaning freshness/softness.
**طراوة** (tarawa) = tenderness/freshness — the abstract noun.
Used for both food texture and fresh produce: **خبز طريّ** (fresh soft bread), **لحم طريّ** (tender meat).
**اللحم طريّ** — equational sentence with masculine noun and masculine adjective.

**لذيذ** (ladhidh) = delicious — from root **لذذ** (l-dh-dh), meaning pleasure/delight.
**لذة** (ladhha) = pleasure/delight — the abstract noun.
**هذا لذيذ** (hadha ladhidh) — masculine because **هذا** refers to something masculine (or unspecified).
Feminine: **لذيذة** (ladhidha) — **هذه الوجبة لذيذة** (this meal is delicious).

**شهيّ** (shahiyy) = tasty/appetizing — from root **شهو** (sh-h-w), meaning desire/appetite.
**شهوة** (shahwa) = appetite/craving — the noun.
**شهيّ** specifically suggests food that awakens the appetite.
Feminine: **شهيّة** (shahiyya) — **الحساء شهيّ جداً** — masculine adjective agrees with masculine **الحساء**.

**تفه الطعم** (tafih at-ta'm) = bland — lit. 'trivial of taste', an إضافة construction.
**تافه** (tafih) = trivial/bland/insignificant — from root **تفه** (t-f-h).
**الطعم** (at-ta'm) = the taste — from root **طعم** (t-'-m) which also gives **طعام** (food) and **مطعم** (restaurant).
Alternative phrase: **لا طعم له** (la ta'm lahu) = it has no taste.

**صحيّ** (sihiyy) = healthy — from root **صحح** / **صحو** (s-h-h/w), meaning health/correctness.
**صحة** (sihha) = health, **صحيح** (sahih) = correct/sound — same root family.
**الفاكهة صحية** (al-fakiha sihiyya) — feminine adjective **صحية** agrees with feminine noun **الفاكهة**.

**غير** (ghayr) = non-/not/other than — a particle that negates an adjective: **غير صحيّ** = unhealthy, **غير صحيح** = incorrect.
**الوجبات السريعة** (al-wajbat as-sari'a) = fast food — **سريع** (sari', fast) with the plural noun.
**غير** is widely used: **غير ممكن** (impossible), **غير مناسب** (inappropriate).

**نباتي** (nabati) = vegetarian — from root **نبت** (n-b-t), meaning to grow/sprout.
**نبات** (nabat) = plant/vegetation, **نباتات** (nabatat) = plants.
Feminine: **نباتية** (nabatiyya) — **أنا نباتية** (I am vegetarian, f.).
**أنا نباتي** — equational sentence: no verb 'to be' needed.

**نباتي صارم** (nabati sarim) = vegan — lit. 'strict vegetarian'.
**صارم** (sarim) = strict/rigorous — from root **صرم** (s-r-m), meaning to cut off/sever.
**هي نباتية صارمة** — both adjectives take feminine form to agree with the feminine subject **هي**.

**مجمّد** (mujammad) = frozen — from root **جمد** (j-m-d), meaning to freeze/solidify.
**جمود** (jumud) = rigidity/stagnation — the abstract noun.
**مجمّد** follows the **مُفَعَّل** pattern — the passive participle of Form II (intensive/causative verbs).
**الطعام المجمّد** (at-ta'am al-mujammad) = the frozen food — adjective following definite noun, also definite.

**عضوي** ('udwi) = organic — from root **عضو** ('-d-w), meaning member/organ.
**عضو** ('udw) = member/organ — as in body organ or club member.
**عضوي** in chemistry/food science means 'organic' (carbon-based or naturally grown).
Feminine: **عضوية** ('udwiyya) — **الخضروات العضوية** (the organic vegetables, f. pl.).

**منزلي الصنع** (manzili as-sun') = homemade — lit. 'home-made' using إضافة: 'domestic of the making'.
**منزلي** (manzili) = home/domestic — adjective from **منزل** (manzil, house/dwelling).
**الصنع** (as-sun') = the making — from root **صنع** (s-n-'), meaning to make/manufacture.
**الخبز المنزلي** = the home bread — adjective **منزلي** after the noun.

**يومي** (yawmi) = daily — adjective/adverb from **يوم** (yawm, day).
**يومياً** (yawmiyan) = daily (adverb) — with the adverbial tanwin ending **ـاً**.
**يوم** (yawm) = day — plural **أيام** (ayyam, days) — a broken plural.
**آكل الفاكهة يومياً** — **يومياً** modifies the verb, placed at the end.

**تقريباً** (taqriban) = almost/approximately — from root **قرب** (q-r-b), meaning nearness.
**قريب** (qarib) = near/close, **تقريب** (taqrib) = approximation — same root.
**الغداء جاهز تقريباً** — the adverb **تقريباً** comes after the predicate adjective **جاهز**.
**تقريباً** is also used for approximations with numbers: **تقريباً عشرون** (approximately twenty).

**أكثر من اللازم** (akthar min al-lazim) = too much — lit. 'more than the necessary'.
**أكثر** (akthar) = more — the comparative of **كثير** (kathir, many/much), using the **أَفْعَل** comparative pattern.
**اللازم** (al-lazim) = what is necessary/required — from root **لزم** (l-z-m), meaning to be obligatory.
**الملح أكثر من اللازم** — the salt is in excess of what is needed.
